title: podman machine start hangs on macOS (x86_64)
body:
### Issue Description

Starting the podman machine VM on macOS Intel Sequoia hangs right after the bootloader screen with the message `Booting 'Fedora CoreOS 41.20250105.3.0 (ostree:0)`. I tried the same on an Apple Silicon device and the VM boots fine, showing EFI statements, then going through its normal boot process.

I tried the Podman version in Homebrew, the offical CLI installer, and installing through Podman Desktop. In all cases it halted immediately after the bootloader screen.

Below are screenshots of the `podman machine start --log-level debug` output and the VM itself showing where it hangs. Console debug output does not contain any errors.

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/770a8145-4904-422a-a4fe-0bea2d690227)
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/830705de-eeb6-4886-a0e9-cac31e6416ef)

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Run `podman machine init`.
2. Run `podman machine start --log-level debug`.
3. VM boot process halts right after the bootloader screen.


### Describe the results you received

The Podman Machine VM bootup process hangs.

### Describe the results you expected

The Podman Machine VM boots.

### podman info output

```yaml
No `podman info` output due to no VM.
Tested with Podman versions 5.3.2 and 5.3.1 with similar results.
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

macOS Sequoia on a Macbook Pro Intel device.

### Additional information

_No response_

provider: applehv
version: 5.3.2

Cannot connect to Podman. Please verify your connection to the Linux system using `podman system connection list`, or try `podman machine init` and `podman machine start` to manage a new Linux VM

```

**EDIT**: I did some testing and the latest working release for me was 5.2.5.

```
CONTAINERS_MACHINE_PROVIDER=applehv podman machine init foo
CONTAINERS_MACHINE_PROVIDER=applehv podman machine start foo
```

CONTAINERS_MACHINE_PROVIDER=libkrun podman machine start kr
Starting machine "kr"
ERRO[0000] process 2253 has not ended
Error: fork/exec /opt/podman/bin/krunkit: bad CPU type in executable
```

Tested with the amd64-only CLI installer as well, and getting the same result.

`podman machine start` worked for me both on macOS 13 and 15 on my intel macbook. However, when I tried `podman --debug machine start` on macOS 15/intel, then it got stuck at "Booting 'Fedora CoreOS ....'"

EDIT: I could only reproduce once :-/

I am having this same issue. Yesterday I removed Docker Desktop and installed podman from the universal DMG and I am seeing the exact results being described above.
System: Macbook Pro 2020, 4 Thunderbolt ports (Intel)

I also tried installing with the `podman-installer-macos-amd64.pkg` from Github which did not resolve the issue. Rebooted multiple times, with no success.

Help would be appreciated

Edit:
I upgraded from Sanoma to Sequoia 15.3 in between troubleshooting. The upgrade solved nothing.
Same here with a 2020 MacBook Pro. Tried everything as the others (rebooting, re-installing, installing only CLI tool, also tried the homebrew version, upgrading OS), nothing fixed it.

I also encountered this issue. As a temporary workaround, I've switched to using the image version 5.2 (5.1 is also working well), which is functioning properly on my Intel-based MacOS:
`podman machine init --cpus=2 --memory=2048 --rootful=true --image=docker://quay.io/podman/machine-os:5.2`

I can confirm all of the above:

* It doesn't matter if you install with Homebrew or the official installer.
* `podman machine reset` doesn't help
* Removing all subdirectories mentioned in `podman machine info` output doesn't help (I held hope for a while that it was the prior VM leaving garbage behind that confused matters.)
* Rebooting doesn't help.

I Initially saw this on upgrading to Podman 5.4.0 with Homebrew on macOS 15.3, but I'm now on the 5.3.2 that Podman Desktop 1.16.1 pulled, under macOS 15.3.1.

It is definitely using a 5.4 CoreOS VM.

```

I am responsible for something like 6 pulls of the CoreOS 41 VM image by now, all due to various attempts to clean things up here and retry.

I will be in the #podman Discord today. Ping me if you need real-time feedback on solution attempts.

All the people with this problem seem to have a 2020 macbook. 
Could you share the output of `sw_vers` and `system_profiler SPHardwareDataType` so that we have a more accurate description of the mac model? You can remove the `Serial Number`, `Hardware UUID`,  `Provisioning UDID` and `Activation Lock Status` from the output as they are unique to your system.

An update:

Same symptoms. 
- podman machine init   # works
- podman machine start  # Prints "Starting machine "podman-machine-default"" and then hangs

Hi I am facing the exact similar problem. Same symptoms. After running `podman machine start ` , it just hangs. 2020 MacBook Pro Processor: 2.3 GHz Quad-Core Intel Core i7 ProductName: macOS ProductVersion: 15.3.1 BuildVersion: 24D70 Hardware: Hardware Overview: Model Name: MacBook Pro Model Identifier: MacBookPro16,2 Processor Name: Quad-Core Intel Core i7 Processor Speed: 2.3 GHz Number of Processors: 1 Total Number of Cores: 4 L2 Cache (per Core): 512 KB L3 Cache: 8 MB Hyper-Threading Technology: Enabled Memory: 32 GB System Firmware Version: 2069.80.3.0.0 (iBridge: 22.16.13051.0.0,0) OS Loader Version: 582~3311 Edited: upgrading Sequoia 15.3 solved nothing.

:)

> [@cfergeau](https://github.com/cfergeau) [@baude](https://github.com/baude) maybe a kernel or disk corruption issue? we have seen something similar before, but that was a long time ago.

Installation of Sequoia over the current Ventura installation: => same issue
Installation of Sequoia on blank HDD: => same issue

Removing podman from MacOs and reinstalling 5.2. using .pkg
* init worked
* default machine started

Docker worked fine all the time....

> maybe a kernel or disk corruption issue?
> ...
> Installation of Sequoia on blank HDD: => same issue

I took this to mean kernel or disk corruption issue _of the image_. Perhaps you can confirm @gbraad?

Poked at this a little today.  Given I don't have the hardware, I'm going to have to ask folks to test some things out.  I went and looked at the images and their kernels.  Two interesting tidbits stick out on the change from 5.2 to 5.3:

1. we changed from fc40 to fc41
2. the kernel changed from 6.11 to 6.12

```
kernel-6.12.10-200.fc41.x86_64  <- 5.5-dev
kernel-6.12.9-200.fc41.x86_64   <- 5.4
kernel-6.12.7-200.fc41.x86_64   <- 5.3
kernel-6.11.3-200.fc40.x86_64   <- 5.2
```

There was considerable difference in fc40 to fc41 but I'm still hoping this is just kernel.  Things we should do ... 

1. Can someone try something like `podman machine init --now --image docker://quay.io/podman/machine-os:5.5` and report back.
2. I have a [dummy PR](https://github.com/containers/podman-machine-os/pull/88) currently building in our CI to see what a new image might bring to the table.  Please pull the correct image from https://github.com/containers/podman-machine-os/pull/88#issuecomment-2679820550 and report back (btw: hint is `--image /path/to/image`)  Edit: I dont think one will be any better than the 5.5 one as it has the same kernel.
3. We bump to a testing version of fcos in the same PR and see if that helps (edit: i did this and the image is building)
4. can someone use vfkit and just boot a fedora cloud image and report the result ?

> can someone use vfkit and just boot a fedora cloud image and report the result ?

For fedora cloud testing, you need a "Raw" Intel/AMD image from https://fedoraproject.org/cloud/download After downloading and uncompressing it, you can start vfkit with a command line such as:
```
vfkit --cpus 2 --memory 2048 \
    --bootloader efi,variable-store=efistore.nvram,create \
    --device virtio-blk,path=$DISKIMAGE \
    --device virtio-serial,logFilePath=vfkit.log \
    --device virtio-net,nat,mac=72:20:43:d4:38:62 \
    --device virtio-rng \
    --device virtio-input,keyboard \
    --device virtio-input,pointing \
    --device virtio-gpu,width=1920,height=1080 \
    --gui
```

Replace `$DISKIMAGE` with the path to the uncompressed fedora cloud image. It will create `efistore.nvram`  and `vfkit.log` files in the current directory and open a window to show the boot process.

> 1. Can someone try something like `podman machine init --now --image docker://quay.io/podman/machine-os:5.5` and report back. I needed a `podman machine reset` first to get rid of the 5.2 OS, but then giving your command hangs it, [as reported 2 weeks ago](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/25121#issuecomment-2660582344). > Please pull the correct image Um, yes, _do the right thing_, got it. :) In my ignorance, I attempted: ``` $ podman machine init --now --image https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/artifact/build/5284591231041536/image_build/image/podman-machine.x86_64.tar ``` That ate a gigabyte of my download cap, then hung without doing anything useful. Going from your hint that I should be giving a local path instead, I went and found that tarball buried under `~/.local`, moved it out to `~/Downloads`, and then made a second attempt: ``` $ podman machine init --now --image ~/Downloads/podman-machine.x86_64.tar Extracting compressed file: podman-machine-default-amd64.raw: done Machine init complete Starting machine "podman-machine-default" Error: vfkit exited unexpectedly with exit code 0 ``` Going from the `vfkit` hint in that error, I then pulled `podman-machine.x86_64.applehv.raw.zst` and retried: ``` $ podman machine reset --force $ podman machine init --now --image ~/Downloads/podman-machine.x86_64.applehv.raw.zst Extracting compressed file: podman-machine-default-amd64.raw: done Machine init complete Starting machine "podman-machine-default" ``` It's been hanging there for many minutes now, having left it trying to start all through the next bit. > can someone use vfkit and just boot a fedora cloud image and report the result ? I pulled [this image](https://download.fedoraproject.org/pub/fedora/linux/releases/41/Cloud/x86_64/images/Fedora-Cloud-Base-AmazonEC2-41-1.4.x86_64.raw.xz), ran `xz -d` on it, and then tried booting it in UTM with the "Use Apple Virtualization" option enabled, but I get this: Whether this is a UTM thing, a QEMU thing, or something else, I'm approaching my limit when it comes to starting this image under vfkit. Is there a CLI command you can send that bypasses UTM for this?
if you use `podman --log-level debug machine ...` do you see a GUI pop up and anything after the kernel starts to boot ?

> if you use `podman --log-level debug machine ...` do you see a GUI pop up and anything after the kernel starts to boot ? I reset it once more, then retried the 5.5 image test with this flag and got the same symptom as reported up-thread. The image pulls, it begins the boot process, and then it hangs in the GUI window: Please belay my request for a `vfkit` command. I didn't see [@cfergeau's message above](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/25121#issuecomment-2681730637) until after I finished replying to yours, but on attempting that, it boots and then gives a blank GUI after I decompressed the zstd image to get a `.raw` file. Nothing more appears, though I've left it running through composing most of this reply.

I found one claim online that I can SSH in as user fedora, but that appears to require some pre-set SSH key, possibly taking me back to the cloud-init path.

If you want to set up a user with cloud-init, you can do this
1. create 2 empty files called `meta-data` and `user-data`
2. open the `user-data` file and copy/paste this
```
#cloud-config
users:
  - name: one
    sudo: ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL
    shell: /bin/bash
    groups: users
    plain_text_passwd: test
    lock_passwd: false  
ssh_pwauth: true
chpasswd: { expire: false }
```

3. create an image containing those two files -> `mkisofs -output seed.img -volid cidata -rock {user-data,meta-data}`
4. append this to your vfkit command -> `--device virtio-blk,path=<full-path>/seed.img`

now you should be able to ssh in using user `one` and psw `test`

If you use the commandline I gave with, you should be able to find the VM IP in `/var/db/dhcpd_leases`, its `hw_address` is `72:20:43:d4:38:62`

The entry looks like this:
```
{
        name=api
        ip_address=192.168.64.2
        hw_address=1,72:20:43:d4:38:62
        identifier=1,72:20:43:d4:38:62
        lease=0x67bef572
}
```

Thanks to @lstocchi's canned cloud-init setup above, I can now kill the working Fedora Cloud image with this script:

```
#!/bin/sh -e
v=$1
s=$2
u=https://kojipkgs.fedoraproject.org/packages/kernel/${v}/${s}.fc41/x86_64
sudo rpm -ivh \
    ${u}/kernel-core-${v}-${s}.fc41.x86_64.rpm \
    ${u}/kernel-modules-core-${v}-${s}.fc41.x86_64.rpm
sudo reboot
```

…which I can call as "`./test 6.12.15 200`" to trigger the non-bootable state.

can someone with one of these Intel models please confirm (or not) that adding nopku to the command line in grub fixes this issue for them? perhaps @tangentsoft

> please confirm (or not) that adding nopku to the command line in grub fixes this issue for them?

It does. Method:

1. `podman machine reset`
2. `podman machine init --log-level debug --now`
3. Wait for the grub menu to come up, then hit `e` in the 2-second window it gives you.
4. Edit the line beginning with "`linux`" and append the "`nopku`" flag.
5. Ctrl-X to boot.

Because this sequence re-pulls the latest OS image, if you shut that machine down and say "`podman machine start --log-level debug`" you get the hang again. Ctrl-C'ing out of that and repeating the `nopku` fix allows the previously-initted machine to start.

> Probably same as [kernel 6.12 doesn't boot (when CONFIG_X86_INTEL_MEMORY_PROTECTION_KEYS is set)](https://github.com/lima-vm/lima/issues/3334#issuecomment-2713802573)

If there is a patch (fix) for this already upstream we may be able to get the fedora kernel maintainer to backport it.

Editing the grub2 config allows for a more "permanent" fix.
To edit the grub2 config, boot up like @tangentsoft mentioned [here](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/25121#issuecomment-2741596761), and then run the following:
```sudo rpm-ostree kargs --editor```

Go down to the end of the last line (this is essentially VI, so down arrow to the last line and then `A` to start editing at the end of the line).  Add ` nopku` and press the ESC key.  Save and exit with `:wq!`

The prompt says to do a reboot, but it's believed better to do a shutdown/poweroff and then start the machine again.  `shutdown -h now` works for that.

When you start the machine `podman machine start` it should boot normally and all the podman commands should work as designed.

Have to give most of the credit to [baude](https://github.com/baude) and Luigi (sorry don't have his GitHub user name).  I was just along for the ride (helped where I could).

> The good mac:

The key detail, which you have not provided, is which CoreOS version your `podman machine` on that Mac is currently using. The fact that it works without help strongly suggests it's the version from Podman 5.2 or earlier. Upgrading Podman doesn't automatically upgrade the "machine" OS. It will keep using it until you `podman machine reset` it.

> Despite the success with `nopku` I still cannot really use it, `podman info` fails as reported earlier:

You're following [this](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/25121#issuecomment-2764487184) procedure, the OS boots, and you _still_ can't get `podman` CLI commands to work on the host?

> The key detail, which you have not provided, is which CoreOS version your `podman machine` on that Mac is currently using. The fact that it works without help strongly suggests it's the version from Podman 5.2 or earlier. Upgrading Podman doesn't automatically upgrade the "machine" OS. It will keep using it until you `podman machine reset` it.

I reset the machines on both the macs, on the good one that does not need `nopku` I have:

Good LORD! The `nopku` option worked for me. But for the love of all that is holy, what is the ETA on this fix? 

I'm on: 
Macbook pro 2020
2.3 GHz Quad-Core Intel Core i7
macOS 15.4

I'm not very knowledgeable on kernel/grub/fedora, so this might be useful for others in my situation.
Editing the command in grub works, but I had to do it every time. To make it permanent, once you finish booting and get to the console as root, you can run `rpm-ostree kargs --append='nopku'` and the argument will be added to the startup sequence. After that you can `start` the machine as normal, without the `--log-level debug` extra.
@frabarz that worked for the podman machine issue. Appreciate it :)
@chrishoina I hear there is a fix coming from the MacOS side.  The `--append=nopku` was just a work around until Mac fixed the issue they created.  At least that's what I recall.
